reviews.author: [ T ] im
reviews.text: Cu Chi Tunnels is an extremely interesting and meaningful place to visit. There are two main sites, Bến Đình and Bến Dược, managed by Cu Chi and the central government (Sai Gon) respectively. While both sites share the same core tour content, such as the historical documentary film, explanations of tunnel structures, different room types, and the reasons the tunnels were built, the overall experience at each site has some differences. At both locations, the tour provides very detailed and vivid information about life underground. You can see how narrow and complex the tunnels are and even crawl through a short section yourself, which really helps you understand how challenging life was. The displays of traps, weapons, and manufacturing processes feel very real, giving a strong sense of how people lived, fought, and survived. Visitors can also try traditional wartime food such as boiled manioc with salt and sesame, and experience firing replica guns similar to those used during that period. At Bến Đình, as a Vietnamese visitor or if you understand Vietnamese at a high level, you only need to buy a normal entrance ticket. There are free guided tours in Vietnamese that run periodically. You just need to ask the staff at the entrance. There is no need to purchase an additional tour guide ticket. This surprised and impressed me quite a lot, as the explanations were detailed and well organized. At Bến Dược, guided tours are provided by default, so the experience feels more structured from the beginning. In addition to the main tunnel tour, Bến Dược offers deeper insight into civilian life before and during the war. One of the most impressive highlights is the large sand table display with lights, smoke effects, and storytelling, showing how the liberation zones were formed, how intense the bombings were, and what people had to endure. I also watched a 3D film that powerfully illustrated the impact of bombs on the land and on human lives. Bến Dược is also home to the Bến Dược Temple, a memorial dedicated to honoring the soldiers and civilians who sacrificed their lives in the resistance wars. Visiting the temple offers a quiet and reflective space to pay respect, read the names and stories of those who were lost, and better understand the human cost behind the history presented in the tunnels. It adds an important emotional and cultural dimension to the visit and helps complete the overall experience. Beyond the historical exhibitions, Bến Dược also has more interactive and recreational activities such as paintball shooting, swimming, and cultural performances, making it suitable for both education and hands on experiences. Overall, everything felt incredibly real and impactful right in front of my eyes. Củ Chi Tunnels is not just a historical site. It is a powerful reminder of resilience, creativity, and survival. I truly loved this place and highly recommend it to anyone who wants to understand an important part of Vietnam’s history.

reviews.author: Dr.Hemendra Agrawal- Knee &Shoulder Doc- ACL expert
reviews.text: Very Visiting the Cu Chi Tunnels was one of the most powerful and eye-opening experiences of my Vietnam trip. This incredible underground network offers a deep insight into the resilience, intelligence, and courage of the Vietnamese people during the Vietnam War. Walking through the site, you truly appreciate how soldiers lived, worked, and survived for years in extremely challenging conditions. The guided tour is informative and engaging, explaining the ingenious tunnel design, hidden trapdoors, ventilation systems, and daily life underground. Crawling through a section of the tunnel—though optional—gives a real sense of how narrow and intense life must have been. The preserved bunkers, command rooms, and war relics make history feel very real and personal. The site is well maintained, organized, and surrounded by greenery, which contrasts sharply with the stories of hardship shared during the tour. Facilities are decent, and guides are knowledgeable and respectful. Overall, the Cu Chi Tunnels are not just a tourist attraction but a moving historical lesson. A must-visit for anyone interested in history, culture, and understanding Vietnam beyond its beautiful landscapes.

reviews.author: Z M
reviews.text: Cu Chi Tunnels are a lot fun. I did a guided tours from Saigon, booked it on Trip.com for $20. Small group of 8 people on bougie bus ride. That way you are not rushed with 40 people with you trying to take photos and do see the same thing. The Tunnels are pretty cool, the tour it self was very informative. If you can fit into all the tunnels, you will see the command Center, the hospital and a sleeping Chambers. They show you all the traps they used to make, how they used to breathe and cook Underground. All the weapons used in the war including missiles and the bombs. They also have a shooting range afterwards which is ridiculously expensive. They charge by the bullet. The tunnels are very intricate, they had a clever system going on at the time. Highly recommended 👌. Watch out for bats down their.

reviews.author: Shabbir Raja
reviews.text: Wow! What an amazing place. If you wish to live History from 1970's this is a must visit place. How thoughtfully planned tunnels! Wonderful warfare tactics to learn and great to know how wise those people were who did this at that time. They were indeed ahead in their knowledge and massive amount of effort they might have put to build something like this. When you visit the place and if you know the history, you can feel the things there. What a vibe!! For history lovers and tourists who are interested in subject of warfare, this is a must visit place. You can't miss it for sure

reviews.author: Kimchi
reviews.text: Xin Chào! I had an incredible experience joining a tour organized by SST Travel. Our tour guide, Kyle, provided clear, engaging explanations throughout the trip. He was knowledgeable, friendly, and very attentive to everyone’s needs. You can easily book this trip through Klook to enjoy the same amazing experience. The journey took 2 hours from Ho Chi Minh City to Chu Chi province. We had the opportunity to enter the tunnel—it's narrow, and you’ll need to bend down for most of the walk. It’s not recommended for anyone who is claustrophobic or has heart-related issues. Experiencing this firsthand made me truly appreciate how the Vietnamese people were able to survive underground for 20 years. Their ability to adapt to their surroundings is nothing short of remarkable and ingenious.

reviews.author: Syafiq Rahman
reviews.text: Found myself a spot on a joining tour to Ben Dinh Cu Chi tunnels. Our tour guide Khang is very knowledgeable and cared for all fifteen of us getting our clips/shots as well as our safety. There were many other visitors but it wasn't super crowded. Really interesting to learn about all the traps used as well as the ways air, food, and water were sustained in the tunnels! Got myself 20 shots on the AK47 at the rifle range (received a free Khan Ran scarf as well) and of course managed my way through the tunnels! There were two tunnels - the first one I had to bend my torso down for the bulk of it. The pictures online were kinda deceiving, I thought I could just walk normally through the tunnels! There is a guide who leads the group too. Mind you that I'm a 1.68m Asian - I saw a bunch of taller visitors leaving within the first 10m of the first set of tunnels as they felt claustrophobic or hot. There is one part towards the end of the first set of tunnels where you have to crawl to get to the end. The second set of tunnels was NOT illuminated, and I had to crawl all the way. I guess this one is closer to the experience of the viet cong soldiers during the war. It was dark, cooling, tight. There were bends and some drops/steps to work through. I don't think I can get another experience like this elsewhere! You can choose to crawl through both tunnels, the first one only, or none. There is also a small cafe near the range, and 2 souvenir shops. Towards the end we were served STEAMED CASSAVA/TAPIOCA WHICH IS SO GOOD I LOVE IT! I highly recommend visiting to immerse yourself in the wartime experience!

reviews.author: Promothes Baruah
reviews.text: Visiting the Cu Chi Tunnels was an unforgettable experience. The guided tour helped me deeply understand the Vietnam War, how the tunnels were used, and the incredible resilience of the people who lived and fought there. Firing the AK-47 was a once-in-a-lifetime experience that added a thrilling and memorable touch to the visit. Overall, it’s an enjoyable, well-organized tourist place that perfectly blends history, learning, and adventure.

reviews.author: Sacha Kaboha
reviews.text: Loved the tour! Very detailed and got to go inside the tunnels from the Vietnam War (not for the claustrophobic!). We west with our children ages 9 and 6 and they loved it. Be prepared for a long day though.

reviews.author: PARTHA BHATTACHARJEE
reviews.text: Located North West around 50 kilometres from Ho Chi Minh airport this is one of the unique, unforgettable and profound places we visited in Vietnam. It takes roughly 1.5 to 2 hours from the airport mainly because of high traffic snarls in the city. Its thought provoking and seriously makes one think about the necessity of peace and calmness required in world today for human race to survive. The booby traps and hidden underground bunkers, reportedly spread in a network, across a total length of 250 kilometres approx, was made self sufficient for the survival of the Vietnamese guerilla soldiers, who were in a do or die situation with American soldiers in the war. The tunnels barely walkable (said to be made wider for the tourists to walk or squeeze in), are severely claustrophobic and had rooms, clinics, kitchens, dining hall, meeting rooms, shooting spots, ammunition storages and what not. The tunnels connected to the river directly to enable the soldiers escape surface and travel directly by water ways. A highly recommended place for visit in the entire Vietnam.

reviews.author: Alexandru Mazilu
reviews.text: I went here with a guided tour which I highly recommend for an enhanced experience. It was interesting to see the ingenuity and resilience of the Vietnamese people. If you are mildly claustrophobic you should skip the tunnels. Even though they are spacious enough they can give you a bit of a panic attack.

reviews.author: Greg Daggett
reviews.text: We had an excellent time with our tour guide Bunny booked as private tour through Viator. $460 USD for people in private limo van. Group non private for $180. I debated converting to non-private and now have no regrets spending a little more. The drive to the tunnels was very informative that we covered hundreds of years in history of Vietnam. We arrived in two hours of driving. Bunny was patient and informative and super helpful. Enjoyed being a small group.. the ride home was quiet and relaxing and just perfect. I would reckon shooting the guns, including the AK47 and the RPD can buy 20 bullets for each. Each bullet cost $3 USD.

reviews.author: Anujith Vijayakumar
reviews.text: Visiting the Cu Chi Tunnels is far more than just a historical tour; it is a profoundly immersive and eye-opening experience that will give you literal goosebumps. The sheer resourcefulness and determination of the Vietnamese people, who carved this complex, 250km-long underground city by hand, is astonishing. ​Seeing the incredibly small, cleverly hidden trapdoor entrances instantly sends a chill down your spine as you realize the ingenuity required for survival. But the ultimate moment is crawling through the tunnels yourself (sections have been widened, but they are still incredibly narrow!). The immediate sense of darkness, heat, and claustrophobia is deeply unsettling and offers a shocking, visceral understanding of the challenging life endured by soldiers and civilians here. ​It is a humbling, powerful, and absolutely essential visit that leaves you in awe of human resilience and the powerful, tangible history under your feet.

reviews.author: Fiona Ong
reviews.text: Here we come to Cu Chi Tunnel. If you going to HCM and love history. dont miss it. Entry fee not expensive also not to worry Once we enter they're tour guide walk us around. we took total 1hrs 45min grab car over from our place. My husband enjoy the guy shooting AK47 also the small tiny Tunnel go down and cover up. I myself try to walk through into a small Tunnel Omg so hot, need to band down to walk. But very good experience. It’s hard to believe how people dug and lived in tunnels during the war. Overall very good experience for us.

reviews.author: Angelica Dalangin
reviews.text: Visiting the Cu Chi Tunnels was one of the most memorable parts of our Vietnam trip. The experience was both eye-opening and deeply moving. Walking through the preserved tunnels and learning about the resilience and ingenuity of the Vietnamese people gave us a whole new perspective on history. Our guide, Wing Wing, explained everything clearly and respectfully, making the stories feel real and personal rather than just facts from a textbook. The site is well-maintained, organized, and educational without feeling overwhelming. If you’re visiting Vietnam, this is definitely a must-see. Highly recommended!

reviews.author: J S
reviews.text: It was a wonderful experience for us to visit Cu Chi tunnel. Our tour guide explained in details how the Viet soldiers survived in the tunnels and about the difficulties faced in the Vietnam War. Going inside the various tunnels is the highlight of the tour. She showed us different traps and tricks used during the war. Outside the area , it’s possible to try AK47 and other guns, but it was very expensive.

reviews.author: Van Dang
reviews.text: The tourist site does an excellent job of bringing history to life; the recreated scenes of the Củ Chi people during the war are so immersive they reminded me of those setups from Universal Studio. Entrance Fee is very cheap too about $2 $4. This’s a unique, hands-on way to understand the resilience of the people who lived here doing the war time... There are coconut juice and snacks half way people can purchase which is a plus. Minus 1 star because we pay extra fee for tour guy, expecting a private tour but it's actually a group tour.

reviews.author: Said Zamzad
reviews.text: What an experience! First time I've first hand experienced a historical moment in modern human history told from the "other side". Truly eye opening and horrific moment in our history, especially with the current geopolitical events occurring live in front of our eyes, it gives a whole new perspective to it. This is a great place for all colonizing countries to visit, especially the French, British and of course Americans.

reviews.author: Sumedh Khare
reviews.text: A great place to visit. They also have an optional chargable gun range shooting where you can fire AK-47 or a pistol or a MP50. Spot booking available. Located 1 hour from HCMC city.

reviews.author: Alan Holmes
reviews.text: Friday 17th October 2025: Good experience to visit and see how the NVA were so aware of their own surroundings and used them to their advantage. Plenty to visit on the site, but it gets extremely busy with tourists, and we were there at the start of the season. You can use the rifle range whilst there: hand gun (cant remember which one), and M16. I was informed they charge $2 per round, so if you want a full clip, thats gonna cost. Enjoyed the visit, it was just too busy, its one of thos early morning jobs you need to be there as soon as it opens to get total enjoyment

reviews.author: Maximilian tourist
reviews.text: This was a beautiful experience. They show you around how it was in the war. You can go through the tunnels where people lived when the Americans came and bombed Vietnam. I learned very much from this excellent experience. I really got to see how tough it was for people to live in those times. They also show the war tactics the Vietnamese used. And the traps they built for the Americans to fall down. That trap with spikes looked painful! And there is also a shooting range which was my favourite part. You can shoot with pistols, assault rifles, machine guns and snipers. Specifically: M60 (belt fed), RPD (belt fed), AK-47, M16, Tokarev, Carabine and M1. Beautiful experience. 5 stars.

reviews.author: Vishal Chokkala
reviews.text: Must visit place, have a look at the horrors of war and the unique guerilla warfare style of the vietnamese people. The tunnels themselves are a bit narrow and claustrophobic, so go inside at your own peril. The shooting range makes a constant gunfire noise, making you feel like you're in the war.

reviews.author: stuart marshall
reviews.text: Went here via an excursion, so we had a guide from the start. He took us through the tunnels, explaining as we continued, what each tunnel was used for not just for fighting. You need to have good knees (see the 1st video) plenty of bent over walking, with low headroom, good idea to wear a hat, just in case of banging your head, which is what I did,ouch, then I put my hat on. Also if want to film your tunnel walking, if possible it might be worth while using a head torch, so you've at least one hand free, because some of the tunnels have no illumination. Well worth the effort.

reviews.author: Christopher Yandell
reviews.text: Highly interactive and educational museum of the tunnels that the Viet Cong soldiers used during the war with the French and the Americans. There are exhibits that show the various traps and guerrilla tactics that soldiers used, how they lived, ate, and remained undetected to American soldiers. I would recommend staying here for at least a couple hours, and the drive is about 2 hours from central Saigon during high traffic times.

reviews.author: Max R-v
reviews.text: Lots of mosquitos. I didn’t go to the big museum I went through the tunnels. Not bad. They had a mig21, a mig17, a C-130, a torn up m48 Patton, a couple destroyed M113 hulls, and some arty pieces. For how far away it is from the city I don’t think it’s worth it.

reviews.author: Alex Lopes
reviews.text: Definitely worth the visit if you are in HCMC, located about 75 minutes from District 1. I recommend getting a local tour to greatly facilitate logistics. Most of what you will see in the area is a reproduction because the original tunnels have mostly collapsed. Still it gives the visitor a very good sense of how the war transpired in that part of the country and how the liberation army survived. We even got to try some of the boiled yuca bites that the soldiers ate. A visit to the tunnels should last between 1.5 and 2 hours but most tours would include other stops.

reviews.author: Michael Cordukes
reviews.text: Great to see the tunnels usd in the Vietnam war. The tour is quite quick but very informative. The experience will depend on the quality of tour company. We also took advandage of the rifle range after the tour. Rifles bolted down, so it's just really pulling the trigger, but still an experience if you haven't shot a gun before

reviews.author: DR. JAGA M. BASANTIA
reviews.text: If you in Vietnam must visit Cu Chi for experiencing the mind blowing war techniques against American. Vietnam is a country that truly captures the heart of anyone who visits. The people of Vietnam are among the most welcoming, humble, and kind-hearted individuals I have ever met. Their warmth and hospitality make visitors feel at home from the very first moment. Whether in the bustling streets of Hanoi or the peaceful villages along the Mekong Delta, there’s always a smile to greet you and a helping hand ready when needed. The culture of Vietnam is equally rich and inspiring—a perfect blend of tradition, resilience, and grace. Deeply rooted in family values, respect for elders, and community spirit, Vietnamese culture reflects a harmony between the past and the present. Festivals, music, and art beautifully showcase the nation’s history and pride, while its cuisine—fresh, flavorful, and balanced—perfectly mirrors the Vietnamese way of life: simple yet deeply satisfying. What stands out most is the people’s positive spirit and their ability to find joy and strength even in challenges. Their respect for nature, tradition, and human connection is truly admirable. Overall, Vietnam’s people and culture leave a lasting impression—a reminder that kindness, simplicity, and unity are the true essence of a beautiful life.

reviews.author: Pubudu Thennakoon
reviews.text: The Củ Chi Tunnels, located near Ho Chi Minh City, are an extensive underground network used by Vietnamese soldiers during the Vietnam War. Stretching over 200 kilometers, these tunnels served as hiding spots, living quarters, supply routes, and hospitals. Today, they are a popular tourist attraction where visitors can explore sections of the tunnels and learn about the resilience and ingenuity of the Vietnamese people during wartime. Great experience and unforgettable day.

reviews.author: Evan
reviews.text: I am torn between admiration for the creativity of the Vietcong at implementing guerrilla warfare, horror at what they went through, terror for the American soldiers, regret for the US military involvement but also appreciation for why the south needed protection. Perhaps most amazing is that now, decades later, the Vietnamese people accept each other and Americans. It’s a complex dance of trauma, politics, forgiveness, and trying to look to the future.

reviews.author: Marc M
reviews.text: It wasn’t the most exciting, our tour guide made it more interesting than the actual place itself. Highlights of the experience had to be the gun range & actually going through some of the tunnels itself. Cost more money to get there than the actual entrance fee with the tour guide the place gave us.
